questions.question: What happens if you omit `break` in a classic switch statement case?
options: Fall-through: execution continues into the next case
questions.explanation: In classic switch statements, omitting break causes fall-through — execution continues into the next case regardless of whether it matches. Arrow syntax (Java 14+) eliminates this problem entirely.

questions.question: Which is a valid switch expression using arrow syntax?
questions.explanation: Switch expressions use -> (arrow) syntax and must be exhaustive (include default or cover all values). They end with a semicolon when used in an assignment statement. The => syntax does not exist in Java.

questions.question: What does `yield` do in a switch expression?
options: Returns a value from a multi-line case block in a switch expression
Exits the switch and returns to the caller method
Throws a SwitchException if no case matches
questions.explanation: In switch expressions, yield returns a value from a multi-line block case. It is only valid inside switch expression blocks and is different from Thread.yield().

questions.question: What does `break outer;` do in nested loops where `outer:` labels the outer loop?
options: Breaks out of both the inner and outer loops
Continues to the next iteration of the outer loop
Compilation error — labels are not allowed on loops
questions.explanation: break with a label exits the labeled loop entirely. When used inside nested loops, `break outer;` exits both loops, jumping past the end of the outer loop.

questions.question: Which Java 21 feature allows type-based branching in switch?
options: Pattern matching in switch with instanceof-style case labels
questions.explanation: Java 21 finalized pattern matching in switch, allowing cases like `case Integer i ->` and `case String s ->` to match by type and bind variables, combining instanceof checking with switch branching.
